items.title: Clinical classification of dehydration
items.contentHtml:
Clinical classification of dehydration

  • Mild dehydration (without signs): <5% loss of body weight in infants, <3% in older children; increased thirst, slightly dry oral mucosa, without alteration of the skin fold or sunken eyes.
  • Moderate dehydration (warning signs): loss 5-10% (infants) or 3-6% (older); sunken eyes, intense thirst, slow skin fold (>2 seconds), dry mucosa, oliguria.
  • Severe dehydration (hypovolemic shock): >10% (infants) or >6% (older); signs of shock: weak or non-palpable pulse, cold extremities, capillary refill >3 seconds, hypotension, lethargy or coma.
  • Gorelick Scale (4 signs: thirst, sunken eyes, slow folding, dry mucosa): 2-3 signs = moderate dehydration; 4 signs = severe.

items.foco: Plan A: prevent dehydration at home
items.title: Plan A: prevent dehydration at home
items.contentHtml:
Plan A: prevent dehydration at home

  • Indicated in acute diarrhea without signs of dehydration (mild).
  • Administer oral rehydration solution (ORS) as needed: <2 years: 50-100 ml for each liquid stool; ≥2 years: 100-200 ml per bowel movement.
  • Continue usual feeding (breastfeeding on demand).
  • Do not suspend breastfeeding or offer sugary or commercial drinks (high osmolarity).
  • Supplement zinc (20 mg/day in ≥6 months, 10 mg/day in <6 months) for 10-14 days to reduce duration and severity.
  • Show the mother warning signs (intense thirst, sunken eyes, persistent vomiting) to consult immediately.

items.title: Plan B: supervised oral rehydration
items.contentHtml:
Plan B: supervised oral rehydration

  • Indicated in moderate dehydration (with alarm signs but without shock).
  • Administer oral rehydration solution in doses of 75 ml/kg in 4 hours, divided into small frequent doses (every 5-10 minutes).
  • Hourly evaluation: vital signs, skin fold, sunken eyes, thirst; if moderate signs persist, repeat the 4-hour cycle.
  • If vomiting: offer 5-10 ml every 2-3 minutes with a spoon or syringe; if they persist, switch to a nasogastric tube (risk of bronchoaspiration).
  • Contraindications for oral rehydration: paralytic ileus, severe abdominal distension, involuntary vomiting, altered state of consciousness.
  • Once dehydration is corrected, move to Plan A (home maintenance).

items.title: Plan C: urgent intravenous rehydration
items.contentHtml:
Plan C: urgent intravenous rehydration

  • Indicated in severe dehydration (hypovolemic shock) or if oral rehydration is contraindicated.
  • First hour: lactated Ringer's solution or isotonic saline (0.9% sodium chloride) at 20 ml/kg as a rapid IV bolus (in 15-30 minutes) in children; repeat bolus if signs of shock persist.
  • Assess perfusion every 15 minutes: capillary refill, pulse, blood pressure; up to 3 boluses in the first hour (maximum 60 ml/kg).
  • Subsequently, complete the estimated deficit: 100 ml/kg for severe dehydration, administered in 2-4 hours (half in the first hour, the rest in 2-3 hours).
  • If venous access is not achieved: intraosseous route in children <6 years, or nasogastric tube with ORS at 20 ml/kg/hour as a bridge.
  • Monitor for signs of fluid overload (crackles, jugular engorgement).

items.title: ORS composition
items.contentHtml:
Oral rehydration solution (ORS) composition

  • Reduced osmolarity ORS (recommended by WHO): glucose 75 mmol/L, sodium 75 mEq/L, potassium 20 mEq/L, chlorine 65 mEq/L, citrate 10 mmol/L; total osmolarity 245 mOsm/L.
  • Glucose facilitates sodium-glucose cotransport in the enterocyte (SGLT1), optimizing the absorption of water and electrolytes.
  • Do not use homemade drinks (water with sugar and salt) due to the risk of errors in concentration (hyper/hyponatremia).
  • In severe cholera, ORS with reduced osmolarity is safe; if there is hypoglycemia, add 5% glucose to the IV solution.
  • ORS is contraindicated in ileus, severe shock (priority Plan C) and altered consciousness.

items.foco: Zinc in acute diarrhea reduces duration
items.title: Zinc in acute diarrhea
items.contentHtml:
Zinc in acute diarrhea reduces duration

  • Zinc sulfate (20 mg/day in children ≥6 months, 10 mg/day in <6 months) administered for 10-14 days reduces the duration of acute diarrhea by 25% and the severity (volume of stools).
  • Mechanism: improves intestinal absorption of water and electrolytes, stabilizes the enterocyte membrane. and modulates the local immune response.
  • Start together with the oral rehydration solution from the first day of diarrhea.
  • Do not exceed the dose: risk of toxicity (nausea, vomiting, metallic taste).
  • In persistent diarrhea (>14 days) zinc is also indicated as an adjuvant.

items.title: Signs of severe dehydration: shock
items.contentHtml:
Signs of severe dehydration: shock

  • Hypovolemic shock due to loss >10% of body weight: weak or non-palpable pulse, tachycardia, cold extremities, capillary refill >3 seconds, hypotension (late phase in children), oliguria/anuria.
  • Neurological signs: lethargy, drowsiness, coma, irritability extreme.
  • Skin fold: disappears very slowly (>3 seconds) or does not disappear (tent sign).
  • Very sunken eyes (marked enophthalmos), dry oral mucosa, absence of tears.
  • Immediate treatment: Plan C (lactated Ringer's bolus or saline 20 ml/kg IV in 15-30 minutes, repeat up to 3 boluses).
  • Do not delay venous access: intraosseous route in children <6 years if peripheral venous failure.

items.foco: Evaluate signs: thirst, folding, sunken eyes
items.title: Evaluate signs: thirst, folding, sunken eyes
items.contentHtml:
Assess signs: thirst, wrinkles, sunken eyes

  • Thirst: early sign of dehydration; the patient drinks greedily (in moderate dehydration) or cannot drink (in severe dehydration due to lethargy).
  • Skin fold: pinching the skin of the abdomen or thigh; normal <1 second; slow 1-2 seconds (moderate); very slow >2 seconds or does not go away (severe). False slowness in severe malnutrition (edema).
  • Sunken eyes: loss of turgor of the eyeball; specific sign of moderate to severe dehydration (sensitivity 60-80%).
  • Combination of these signs improves diagnostic accuracy: Gorelick scale (4 signs: thirst, sunken eyes, slow folding, dry mucosa) ≥2 signs = moderate dehydration.

items.title: Oral rehydration contraindications
items.contentHtml:
Contraindications of oral rehydration

  • Paralytic ileus or severe abdominal distension: risk of vomiting and aspiration; oral rehydration solution is not absorbed and may worsen distension.
  • Uncontrollable vomiting (more than 4 episodes/hour) that prevents oral intake; try a nasogastric tube if there is no ileus.
  • Altered state of consciousness (lethargy, coma): risk of aspiration; requires intravenous route.
  • Hypovolemic shock (severe dehydration): prioritize Plan C with intravenous fluids; Oral rehydration is ineffective due to poor splanchnic perfusion.
  • Intestinal obstruction or perforation: absolute contraindication.

items.title: Maintain feeding during acute diarrhea
items.contentHtml:
Maintain feeding during acute diarrhea

  • Do not suspend breastfeeding: it provides antibodies, trophic factors and reduces the duration of diarrhea.
  • In older children, continue the usual diet (cereals, potatoes, lean meats, yogurt) avoiding simple sugars (juices, soft drinks) that increase luminal osmolarity.
  • Early feeding (within 4-6 hours) reduces the duration of diarrhea and improves the condition. nutritional.
  • In moderate dehydration (Plan B), restart feeding once the dehydration is corrected (after 4 hours of supervised oral rehydration).
  • Avoid foods with high osmolarity (commercial soups, jellies) and laxatives (prunes, prune juice).

items.foco: Antibiotics only in dysenteric diarrhea or cholera
items.title: Antibiotics only in dysenteric diarrhea or cholera
items.contentHtml:
Antibiotics only in dysenteric diarrhea or cholera

  • Most acute diarrhea is viral (rotavirus, norovirus) or self-limiting bacterial; do not prescribe routine antibiotics.
  • Dysentery (visible blood in stool, fever, tenesmus): treat with ceftriaxone (50 mg/kg/day IM/IV for 5 days) or azithromycin (10 mg/kg/day for 3 days) as first line in children.
  • Cholera (massive watery diarrhea, appearance “rice water”): azithromycin single dose 20 mg/kg (maximum 1 g) or doxycycline 300 mg single dose (in adults and children >8 years).
  • In resistant cholera: alternatives such as ciprofloxacin (15 mg/kg/day for 3 days) or erythromycin.
  • Do not use antidiarrheals. (loperamide) in children <6 years due to risk of paralytic ileus and toxicity.

items.title: Prevention: breastfeeding and rotavirus vaccine
items.contentHtml:
Prevention: breastfeeding and rotavirus vaccine

  • Exclusive breastfeeding during the first 6 months reduces the incidence and severity of rotavirus diarrhea and other enteric infections (IgA immunoglobulins, lactoferrin).
  • Rotavirus vaccine (monovalent or pentavalent): oral administration at 2 and 4 months (monovalent) or 2, 4 and 6 months (pentavalent); first dose before 15 weeks of age.
  • Vaccine contraindication: history of intussusception, severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID).
  • Handwashing with soap and water, drinking water, basic sanitation and hygienic food preparation.
  • Prophylactic use of zinc or probiotics is not recommended in healthy children.
